headline: Photography Reveals Mangroves' Hidden Beauty and Urgent Peril
meta_description: The 12th annual Mangrove Photography Awards showcase ecosystems covering just 0.1% of Earth but storing vast carbon and facing collapse by 2050.
summary_short: The Mangrove Action Project's 12th annual photography awards celebrate these vital but overlooked ecosystems—and reveal the threats they face.
body:
The Mangrove Action Project has announced winners of its 12th annual photography awards in celebration of World Mangrove Day, using compelling images to shift how people perceive one of Earth's most misunderstood ecosystems. Though mangroves cover just 0.1% of the planet's surface, they stabilize coastlines, shelter fish, birds, primates and tigers, and store three to five times more carbon per acre than tropical forests. Yet scientists warn that more than half of all mangrove ecosystems risk collapse by 2050 due to climate change—a crisis the award-winning photographs bring into sharp focus.

The contest's overall winner, captured by Indian photographer Gaurav Patil near Nandgaon village south of Mumbai, documents ghost nets—discarded or lost fishing gear—tangled in mangrove roots. At sea, such nets trap sea turtles, birds and whales; their impact on mangrove ecosystems remains poorly understood. Equally striking are photos from the Philippines showing plastic accumulation and from Central Java, Indonesia, depicting a community consumed by rising seas. Yet one image stands out for hope: it features Pasijah, a resident who refused to leave her flooded home and has spent two decades replanting roughly 15,000 mangrove seedlings each year.

The portfolio also celebrates the extraordinary wildlife dependent on mangroves—from lemurs in Madagascar (at least 23 species) to silky anteaters napping in Trinidad's Caroni Swamp to juvenile Bengal tigers in India's Sundarbans. As MAP's creative director noted, photography can change the narrative around these overlooked ecosystems, revealing their fragility and stunning diversity in ways that inspire protection rather than indifference.
quick_facts: 12th annual Mangrove Photography Awards announced by Mangrove Action Project
Mangroves cover 0.1% of Earth but store 3–5× more carbon than tropical forests
Over 50% of mangrove ecosystems at risk of collapse by 2050
Winner shows ghost nets threatening mangroves near Mumbai, India
Indonesian resident planted 15,000 mangrove seedlings yearly for 20 years
